# Digital twins: Boosting ROI of government infrastructure investments

### 

**Consider a scenario** in which state government leaders have three generational transit projects they could invest in but enough capital to support only one. Each project carries distinct benefits as well as unique trade-offs. As they evaluate options, leaders struggle to balance the risks and rewards, given the many competing priorities across the state. Moreover, with each option having a high, irreversible capital cost, making a suboptimal decision is expensive. To navigate this uncertainty, leaders build a digital replica of each project that models all key factors, such as capital cost, potential transit modalities, citizen accessibility, carbon emissions, and energy consumption. They then simulate numerous population growth scenarios years into the future and analyze each optionâs current and future impact. The outputs of these analyses clarify the course of action that maximizes societal benefit and the overall ROI, and the leaders confidently announce their decision.

## About the authors

This article is a collaborative effort by [Alastair Green](/our-people/alastair-green), [Alex Cosmas](/our-people/alex-cosmas), and [Gayatri Shenai](/our-people/gayatri-shenai), with Dylan Moore and Nehal Mehta, representing views from McKinseyâs Public Sector Practice.

Digital twins1Digital twins are virtual replicas of a system, asset, process, or design used to drive data-based decision-making. For more, see â[What is digital-twin technology?](/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-digital-twin-technology),â McKinsey, August 26, 2024. are powerful tools that can dramatically improve the speed and quality of complex decisions like the transit investment above. Recent advancements in computing power and data availability mean that digital twins can now be used to enable government leaders to build accurate digital replicas of real-world assets and systems. These digital twins can enable accurate modeling, scenario planning, and analysis of both the direct and indirect effects of a projectâand some digital twins are already implemented and doing just that. Ultimately, they enable better, faster decision-making and a higher ROI. As such, digital twins can enable governments to implement a âdig onceâ policy, in which potential issues and bottlenecks are identified before a project begins, thereby minimizing disruption to affected populations and decreasing both costs and the risk of delays.2â[âDig onceâ could help states manage material and worker shortages](/industries/public-sector/our-insights/dig-once-could-help-states-manage-material-and-worker-shortages),â McKinsey, August 24, 2022.

The number of active large-scale public infrastructure projects of this type is growing quickly around the world as a result of several landmark investment programs,3Steffen Fuchs, Homayoun Hatami, Tip Huizenga, and Christoph Schmitz, â[Capital investment is about to surge: Are your operations ready?](/capabilities/operations/our-insights/capital-investment-is-about-to-surge-are-your-operations-ready),â McKinsey, April 7, 2022.  including the $1.2 trillion Bipartisan Infrastructure Law in the United States,4âH.R.3684 - Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act,â US Congress, November 15, 2021. the more than â¬800.0 billion NextGenerationEU COVID-19 recovery plan in the European Union,5âRecovery plan for Europe,â European Commission, accessed June 3, 2025.  and the Indian National Infrastructure Pipeline, which contains 1,110 projects under development for a total project cost of more than $2.3 trillion.6âNational Infrastructure Pipeline,â India Investment Grid, accessed March 11, 2025. The success of investment programs such as these is essential because they form a cornerstone of governmentsâ response to myriad ongoing global disruptions. These disruptions include rising geopolitical tensions, stagnating economic growth, the accelerating impact of climate change, growing populations, and rapid developments in AI.

However, translating a major influx of funds into action can be challenging. Access to the right expertise7Zak Cutler and Sam Linder, â[Capital projects are critical for a green future](/industries/infrastructure/our-insights/capital-projects-are-critical-for-a-green-future),â McKinsey, August 15, 2023.  and strong governance in capital planning8Matt Banholzer, Ashish Chandarana, and David Straden, â[Nine practices for better capital-investment management](/capabilities/operations/our-insights/nine-practices-for-better-capital-investment-management),â McKinsey, May 5, 2017.  are critical, but they are not enough. Generating a good ROI also requires a sophisticated approach to planning and execution. Historically, however, fully modeling the real-world impacts of complex capital investments has been too complex for standard modeling approaches, which typically use defined mathematical relationships capturing cause and effect and therefore lack the ability to handle the multiple interdependencies within complex systems. It is in complex decisions with numerous interdependencies that digital twins are most effective, so their value to government leaders can be significant.

This article focuses on maximizing return on capital-intensive infrastructure investments, though there are many other use cases for digital twins, including redesigning complex processes, aiding annual budgeting or acquisition decisions, accelerating product development through deep learning surrogates,9*McKinsey Talks Operations Blog*, â[Using AI to supercharge R&D: Takeaways from the R&D Leaders Forum](/capabilities/operations/our-insights/operations-blog/using-ai-to-supercharge-r-and-d-takeaways-from-the-r-and-d-leaders-forum),â blog entry by Elia Berteletti and Kimberly Borden, July 25, 2024.  and increasing the resilience of supply chains.

Successfully rolling out a digital twin can take significant investment and time, but the ROI through accelerated decision-making and efficient resource allocation can more than justify the cost. In our experience, digital twins have the potential to improve capital efficiency, accessibility of services, and operational performance of public sector investments by 20 to 30 percent.10Typical uplift based on our experience using digital twins to support investment decisions across public sector services and transit infrastructure. When mapped to millions or billions of dollars in expenditure, this payback can be significant.

## Government leaders face unique challenges in making high-impact investments

Given their size and complexity, planning and implementing large-scale infrastructure projects is always a challenge. However, for public sector projects, these difficulties can be compounded and new challenges can be introduced.

Government infrastructure investment projects typically have to grapple with most or all of the following issues:

## Digital twins can be very effective in supporting decision-making

Given the scale and potential of government infrastructure projectsâas well as the challenges involved in getting them rightâdata-driven, proactive, and accurate decision-making is vital.

Recent technological developments mean that digital-twin technologies can now provide exactly that: These products are already being used to drive visualizations, simulations, and optimizations of the physical asset to support decision-making.12*Tech: Forward Blog*, â[Digital twins: When and why to use one](/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/tech-forward/digital-twins-when-and-why-to-use-one),â McKinsey, April 30, 2024. They allow the user to model âwhat is,â âwhat if,â and âwhat should be.â Digital twins typically consist of five elements that can be progressively built over time (Exhibit 1). Process or data flow maps and data modelsâthe first two elementsâare the foundation. Together, they make up a digital replica of a process, integrating the different streams of data and process information that need to be analyzed. These inputs may come from live data feeds, or they may be static, depending on the existing technology stack.

![Digital twins have several core elements, but the primary focus is typically on simulation and emulation.](/~/media/mckinsey/industries/public%20and%20social%20sector/our%20insights/digital%20twins%20boosting%20roi%20of%20government%20infrastructure%20investments/svgz-mck250598%20government%20digital%20twins%20exhibits%20v7-expanded_ex1.svgz?cq=50&cpy=Center)

Image description:

A conceptual exhibit shows the five core elements—or layers—of a digital twin:

Of these layers, the simulation and emulation layers are the core focus of digital twin development.

End of image description.

The third elementâthe emulation layerâcontextualizes the historical and real-time information (that is, seeing what exists in the system today) contained in the data model, enabling a birdâs-eye view or synthesis of the current state of a system (in other words, âwhat isâ). This capability also enables quality control of the underlying model approach and assumptionsâfor example, through simulating known scenarios and comparing model outputs with historical data. This quality control is essential before the digital twin is used to look at future-state or âwhat ifâ scenarios.

The power of digital twins to significantly improve decision-making typically comes when models shift from emulation to simulation, which is the fourth element of digital-twin technology. With this element in place, the user can simulate what-if scenarios that assess the impact of different decisions or different futures on the overall outcome, thereby identifying âwhat should be.â

For example, consider a complex engine-maintenance process that requires a significant amount of equipment and personnel. Once the process is modeled in a digital twin, it becomes possible to quantify bottlenecks across the process instead of relying on intuition or one-off analyses. The digital twin also allows the user to see the results of possible future scenarios, such as comparing the impact of buying new equipment that could speed up a particular process step with the impact of hiring more personnel who could support multiple steps in the process.

The power of digital twins to significantly improve decision-making typically comes when models shift from emulation to simulation.

Some digital twins also include a fifth element: the optimization layer. Rather than identifying the best interventions through trial and error, an optimization layer automatically recommends interventions based on previously identified criteria, which could include, for example, maximizing throughput or minimizing downtime. However, for some twins, the run time for a single model can be up to several hours, which means that availability of computational power can limit the ability to implement a first-best optimization layer. Running the model iteratively 1,000 times to identify the optimal outcome using a hill-climbing algorithm, for instance, could take weeks or months and may not be practical to inform decision-making. In these cases, the user may choose to run a finite set of scenarios through the model to find a course of action that meets predefined goals.

Overall, digital twins are intended to be reused over time to optimize investments and promote organizational effectiveness. They are therefore often considered a âsystem of innovationâ within an organization and are a new paradigm shift alongside AI or machine learning models and gen AI tools (Exhibit 2). As such, digital twins are a key ingredient in future-proof technology architectures.

![Digital twins are a key ingredient in a future-proof technology architecture.](/~/media/mckinsey/industries/public%20and%20social%20sector/our%20insights/digital%20twins%20boosting%20roi%20of%20government%20infrastructure%20investments/svgz-mck250598%20government%20digital%20twins%20exhibits%20v7-expanded_ex2.svgz?cq=50&cpy=Center)

A conceptual exhibit shows the illustrative data infrastructure and integration platform that would constitute a future-proof technology architecture for an organization. The data infrastructure and integration platform consists of four stacked systems, each of which has 1 to 4 elements, which are represented by boxes within the layer:

These four systems exist within a compute environment, which contains standardized infrastructure of data storage, access, and application integration.

End of image description.

## How digital twins are already creating value for capital-intensive infrastructure investments

Digital twins provide the ability to emulate current states and simulate potential future states, offering a powerful tool for modeling the largest, most important, and most complex systems and processes. The most natural use case of digital twins may be to inform large investments in instances when traditional analytical modeling is not sufficient or when prior efforts to model or improve a complex process have not resulted in anticipated returns.

These new technologies can improve decision-making in a number of waysâincluding providing end-to-end visibility across a process or operation, creating a single source of truth, enabling rapid and low-cost scenario analysis, increasing decision-making speed, and mitigating the impact of potential side effects. As a result, digital twins are already being used by government agencies to guide capital investment decisions across multiple domains and at various scales.

***Targeting capital investments to reduce cost and increase energy resilience for government facilities***. A large government organization built a digital twin with advanced optimization and analytical capabilities, including capital project and power-sourcing optimization engines, to evaluate the highest-ROI investments in energy efficiency, cost savings, and resilience. After considering tens of thousands of possible investments, the organization identified a path to more than $100 million in potential savings while also improving resilience.

***Providing end-to-end system visibility to improve urban planning in Australia.*** The state of Victoria, Australia, is integrating real-time data and advanced spatial technologies to create a digital twin of key elements of its physical and social infrastructure. The program aims to provide value by reducing red tape, improving government services, and building more-resilient communities through enhanced disaster response.13âDigital Twin Victoria: About the program,â Victoria State Government, updated June 19, 2024.

***Accelerating and optimizing decision-making for military force design.*** A government agency used digital-twin technologies to optimize investments in military capability in response to an evolving threat. The agency was able to accelerate force modernization by increasing the focus on priority projects and optimizing training schedules within the set budget envelope and desired mission outcomes.

***Modeling global inventory to guide acquisitions and manage risks.*** Government leaders are using digital twins to guide at-scale procurement or acquisition efforts that are heavily influenced by nongovernmental entities. One government entity built a digital twin of aspects of its supply chain to model global inventory levels for key materiel, identify schedule and production bottlenecks, and proactively mitigate logistics disruptions in the system from, for example, the loss of a supplier.

***Mapping complex operational interdependencies and identifying bottlenecks.*** Government leaders are using digital twins to inform investments in complex supply chains and operational processes in instances when analytical modeling is not sufficient to capture interdependencies and dynamics between stakeholders. One government entity built a digital twin to identify key limiting factors related to infrastructure and personnel within a highly complex process that had never been fully mapped.

***Streamlining operations and reducing flight delays at a large airport.*** Leadership at an airport shifted from reactive to proactive planning by using a digital twin to model operations and to identify improvement levers and optimize investments to address rising flight delays. The model allowed the airport to balance the cost of additional staff and equipment with the operational improvement, leading to an overall 20 to 30 percent efficiency improvement.

While digital twins can significantly improve capital investment allocation in many ways and across multiple use cases, the common theme is an increase in ROI. Users of these digital twins can optimize throughput, cost, and safety months or years before buying new assets, investing in new capabilities, hiring more personnel, or breaking ground on new projects, saving precious capital and time.

## Getting started

Building a digital twin requires a deep understanding of the process or system to be modeled, the technical expertise to aggregate data and represent a process appropriately in digital form, and the computational power to run and maintain the model.

While the details of the appropriate digital twin will vary across problem statements and organizations, the broad steps for developing and building the digital twin are generally the same (Exhibit 3).

![There is a standard implementation approach for a digital twin.](/~/media/mckinsey/industries/public%20and%20social%20sector/our%20insights/digital%20twins%20boosting%20roi%20of%20government%20infrastructure%20investments/svgz-mck250598%20government%20digital%20twins%20exhibits%20v7-expanded_ex3.svgz?cq=50&cpy=Center)

Image description:

A conceptual flow exhibit shows the seven steps of a standard implementation approach for a digital twin.

Feedback should be gathered from end-users throughout this development process.

End of image description.

Early steps focus on establishing the scope and goals of the model, as well as on building the data layer. While data availability, complexity, and cost can be an issue, a majority of data can often be pulled from enterprise resource planning systems and integrated with other external sources. Simplifying assumptions are often incorporatedâstitching together imperfect data is typicalâand a road map can be developed to refine the model over time as new data is collected.

Successfully rolling out a digital twin can take significant investment and time, but the ROI through accelerated decision-making and efficient resource allocation can more than justify the cost.

Once the data layer is complete, the emulation and simulation logic can then be built out. Last, the model should be thoroughly tested for validity before being scaled across the organization as needed.

While successfully rolling out a digital twin can take significant investment and time, the return on this investment can be substantial, given the magnitude of current and forthcoming government investments in infrastructure and capabilitiesâand given the value in making effective decisions the first time around. Digital twins could offer governments a real opportunity to ensure that they are deploying their finite resources in a way that accelerates the energy transition, increases economic growth, builds resilience, and helps to navigate global disruptions.

Highlights
----------

*   •Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. 
*   •The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in many industries. 
*   •The benefits of creating a Digital Twin solution are too vast and still not fully explored. 
*   •Digital Twins will combine with more technologies, such as augmented reality (AR) and AI for better connections, insights, and analytics. 

Abstract
--------

Applications of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been growing at an exponential rate, and it is transforming the way businesses operate. In the past few years, [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leveraged vital [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is predicted that the technology will expand to more applications, use cases, and industries. The purpose of this paper is to do a literature review and explore how Digital Twins streamline intelligent automation in different industries. This paper defines the concept, highlights the evolution and development of Digital Twins, reviews its key enabling technologies, examines its trends and challenges, and explores its applications in different industries.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Digital Twin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is attracting attention from practitioners and scholars alike. Today, the technology is used across many industries to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. In 2019, a Gartner survey revealed that Digital Twins were entering mainstream use by organizations. It predicted that 75 percent of [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) organizations also use [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or plan to use it by 2020 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b2). Gartner also estimates that by 2027, over 40 percent of large companies worldwide will be using Digital Twin in their projects to increase revenue [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b3).

Moreover, Global Market Insight estimated that the Digital Twin market size estimated in 2022 at $8 billion is expected to grow at around 25 percent [Compound Annual Growth Rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about Compound Annual Growth Rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAGR) from 2023 to 2032 [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b4). Finally, according to another recent report by global technology research, the Digital Twin market is set to grow by nearly $32 billion from 2021 to 2026 [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b5). In addition, according to a 2022 report, nearly 60 percent of executives across a broad spectrum of industry plan to incorporate Digital Twins within their operations by 2028 [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6).

Digital Twin is a cutting-edge technology that has revolutionized the industry by mirroring almost every facet of a product, process, or service. It has the potential to replicate everything in the physical world in the digital space and provide engineers with feedback from the virtual world [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7). As a result, the technology enables companies to quickly detect and solve physical problems, design and build better products, and realize value and benefits faster than previously possible. Furthermore, the Digital Twin technology enables businesses to improve business processes and performance [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b8).

The research started by developing key concepts based on a narrative literature review in the Scopus database and Google Scholar and searched for papers that included Digital Twin or Digital Twins in the title. Articles were selected from Journal articles, research articles, conference publications, research reports, or scientific encyclopedias. We reviewed the documents to find answers to the following questions: What is a Digital Twin, what technologies are used, what are the current applications of Digital Twins in different industries, and what are the challenges and opportunities facing this growing technology? We selected research papers that defined the concept, reviewed its related technologies, and highlighted its applications and trends in different industries. Section[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec2) provides the main perspectives and definitions of Digital Twins in literature. Section[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec3) reviews four enabling technologies of the Digital Twins. Section[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec4) studies Digital Twin applications and use cases of different industries. Section[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec5) highlights the challenges and opportunities of this technology. Finally, Section[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec6) provides a summary and conclusions. Section 7 provides references.

2. Background and definition
----------------------------

In 2003 Professor Grieves of the University of Michigan introduced the concept of [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a total [product lifecycle management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/product-lifecycle-management "Learn more about product lifecycle management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") course. It is also known as a digital mirror and digital mapping. Since then, its definition has continued to evolve as several scholars have provided varied definitions of this technology [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b14). Encyclopedia of Production Engineering states that “_The Digital Twin is a representation of an active unique “product” which can be a real device, object, machine, service, intangible asset, or a system consisting of a product and its related services”_[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12)_._ In general, the Digital Twin is defined as virtual representations of [physical objects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical objects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the lifecycle that can be understood, learned, and reasoned with real-time data or a simulation model that acquires data from the field and triggers the operation of physical devices [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b16).

Furthermore, Digital Twin was defined as the convergence between physical and virtual products [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b18). Fu et al. [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7) thought of the Digital Twin as a real-time digital representation of a physical object. They are remotely connected to real objects and provide rich representations of these objects. They go beyond static product designs, like CAD models, but comprise dynamic behavior [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b20). A virtual replica of a real-world asset is obtained through constant data transmission allowing the digital version of the object to exist simultaneously with the physical one [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17). Digital Twin uses [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology to mine hidden and effective data and to improve the intelligence and applicability of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), especially for quick identification and evaluation of design flaws [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b21).

Finally, Kritzinger et al. [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13) defined Digital Twin, within the field of manufacturing, based on the data integration level, which can be achieved between the physical product and its virtual representation. He identified three levels of integration, The Digital Model, Digital Shadow and the Digital Twin ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig1)) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b22).

A historic early application of Digital Twin technology is when NASA engineers used a simulator, a twin of the command module, and a separate twin of the module’s [electrical system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrical-system "Learn more about electrical system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to remedy and save Apollo 13 in 1970. NASA engineers completed the process in under two hours and saved the lives of the three astronauts on board. This was an extraordinary early application of this technology, and the technology has only matured since then [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b23). Today, NASA uses Digital Twins to develop next-generation vehicles and aircraft.

The concept of Digital Twins is not new. However, Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. It is predicted that Digital Twins will be combined with more technologies such as speech capabilities, [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [IoT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about IoT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [artificial intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about artificial intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI). As a result, Gartner included Digital Twins on its list of top 10 technology trends for 2017 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Gartner also predicted that half of the large industrial firms to use Digital Twins in crucial [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by 2021 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Finally, MarketsandMarkets research predicted rapid growth for the Digital Twin technology within the next few years, thanks to rising interest in the manufacturing industry to reduce cost and improve [supply chain operations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/supply-chain-operation "Learn more about supply chain operations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, the market for Digital Twin technology was valued at $6.9 billion in 2022. However, it is expected to reach $73.5 billion by 2027- a [CAGR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about CAGR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of more than 60 percent [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b25).

3. Digital twin technologies
----------------------------

The three main aspects of Digital Twins are data acquisition, [data modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-modeling "Learn more about data modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and data application [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). Digital Twin uses four technologies to collect and store real-time data, obtain information to provide valuable insights, and create a digital representation of a [physical object](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical object from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These technologies include the Internet of Things (IoT), [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI), [Extended Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR), and Cloud ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig2)). In addition, Digital Twin uses a particular technology, depending on the application type, to a greater or lesser extent.

*   1._Internet of Things (IoT):_ IoT refers to a giant network of connected “things”. The connection is between things–things, people–things, or people–people [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Digital Twins use IoT as its primary technology in every application. By 2027, more than 90 percent of all IoT platforms will have Digital Twinning capability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6). IoT uses sensors to collect data from real-world objects. The data transmitted by IoT is used to create a digital duplication of a physical object. The digital version then can then be analyzed, manipulated, and optimized. IoT constantly updates data and helps Digital Twin applications create a real-time virtual representation of a physical object. Therefore, every Digital Twin application uses IoT as a primary technology. 
*   2._[Cloud Computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/cloud-computing "Learn more about Cloud Computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_: Cloud computing refers to delivering hosted services over the Internet. The technology efficiently stores and accesses data over the Internet [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Cloud computing provides Digital Twins with data computing technology and cloud data storage technology. Cloud computing allows Digital Twin, with large volumes of data, to store data in the virtual Cloud and easily access the required information from any location. Cloud computing enables Digital Twins to effectively reduce the [computation time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computation-time "Learn more about computation time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of complex systems and overcome the difficulties of storing large amounts of data [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b28). 
*   3.[Artificial Intelligence (AI)](https://softengi.com/expertise/artificial-intelligence/): As a discipline of computer science, AI seeks to mimic the basis of intelligence to create a new intelligent machine capable of responding like human-to-human intelligence. Areas of AI study include Robotics, image recognition, and language recognition. [Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/neural-network "Learn more about Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/expert-system "Learn more about expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b29), AI can assist Digital Twins by providing an advanced analytical tool capable of automatically analyzing obtained data and providing valuable insights, making predictions about outcomes, and giving suggestions as to how to avoid potential problems [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). 
*   4.[Extended Reality (XR)](https://softengi.com/expertise/expanded-reality/) is an umbrella term used to describe immersive technologies like Virtual Reality (VR), [Augmented Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about Augmented Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR), and Mixed Reality (MR). These technologies can merge the physical and virtual worlds and extend the reality we experience [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b30). XR creates digital representations of objects where digital and real-world objects co-exist and interact in real-time. Digital Twins utilize XR capabilities to digitally model physical objects, allowing users to interact with digital content.

4. Digital twins use cases and applications
-------------------------------------------

Today, engineering and manufacturing predominantly use Digital Twins to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. Digital Twins applications in operations and supply chain management, especially the role of Digital Twins in terms of operations traceability, transport maintenance, remote assistance, asset visualization, and design [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are reviewed in related publications [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38). The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in other industries, including automotive, aerospace, construction, agriculture, mining, utilities, retail, healthcare, military, [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and public safety sectors ([Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig3)) [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b44). The technology has captured the imagination of scholars, managers, and practitioners worldwide, and numerous [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of this technology are emerging in the literature [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b47), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b48), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b49), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b50).

Top 5 Benefits of Digital Twin Technology Revolutionising Construction
======================================================================

Digital twin technology is transforming industries, and construction is no exception. But what is a digital twin? At its core, a digital twin is a virtual replica of a physical asset or system, such as a building, infrastructure, or equipment, which is dynamically updated with real-time data. This powerful technology allows construction professionals to create a digital mirror of their projects, enabling them to visualise, simulate, and analyse various components throughout the asset’s lifecycle.

The use of digital twin technology in construction is revolutionising how projects are planned, built, and managed. Below are five transformative benefits of implementing digital twin technology in construction.

1. Enhanced Project Visualisation and Simulation
------------------------------------------------

One of the most significant advantages of digital twin technology in construction is its ability to enhance project visualisation and simulation. Unlike traditional 2D blueprints or static 3D models, digital twins offer dynamic, real-time models that evolve with the project. This provides stakeholders with a comprehensive view of the construction process, from the design phase through to completion and operation.

Moreover, advanced 3D visualisation enables clients to “experience” the project before it’s built. This fosters better client engagement, offering a tangible preview of the finished structure and ensuring that expectations are met, if not exceeded.

2. Improved Collaboration and Communication
-------------------------------------------

Construction projects often involve numerous stakeholders, including architects, engineers, contractors, and clients. Digital twins technology significantly enhances collaboration by providing a unified platform where all parties can access the same real-time data and insights. This centralisation of information minimises misunderstandings, enhances communication, and ensures that everyone is aligned on the project’s progress.

Decision-making becomes more data-driven. Stakeholders can collaborate on simulations to test various construction approaches, identify risks early, and agree on the best course of action.

3. Better Predictive Maintenance and Asset Management
-----------------------------------------------------

Digital twins are not only valuable during the construction phase, but they also offer immense benefits for long-term asset management. Once a building or infrastructure is completed, the digital twin continues to function as a live model that tracks the asset’s performance over time. This makes it easier for facility managers and building owners to monitor and manage operations, maintenance, and repairs.

Additionally, by integrating with Building Information Modelling (BIM) systems, digital twins ensure that accurate information is always available for future renovations or upgrades.

4. Increased Efficiency and Reduced Costs
-----------------------------------------

Digital twins contribute to significant cost savings and increased efficiency throughout the construction lifecycle. By allowing real-time analysis and simulation, construction teams can identify potential issues early on, reducing the likelihood of costly errors during the build phase. The insights gathered from digital twins enable better resource allocation, helping to minimise material waste, optimise labour costs, and streamline workflows.

Digital twins provide the ability to track inventory, materials, and equipment in real time, ensuring that resources are used efficiently and on schedule.

5. Sustainability and Environmental Impact Reduction
----------------------------------------------------

Sustainability is becoming a top priority in construction, and digital twin technology is playing a crucial role in helping the industry reduce its environmental footprint. By optimising resource use, improving energy efficiency, and minimising waste, digital twins contribute to greener building practices.

The real-time data provided by digital twins also helps construction teams track and reduce waste during the building process. By having precise insights into material usage and construction progress, teams can prevent over-ordering materials, reducing excess waste that would otherwise end up in landfills.

##

Here's a surprising fact: the global [digital twin market](https://www.toobler.com/blog/emerging-digital-twin-market) is predicted to rise at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 60.6% to reach $73.5 billion by 2027.

However, why is this technology so prevalent?

[Digital twins](https://www.toobler.com/blog/what-is-digital-twin) provide unparalleled potential for your industry—healthcare, manufacturing, or insurance through:

Cost savings

Resolving practical issues

Operational efficiency

Increased productivity

Improved decision-making,

In this blog, we will go over the main benefits of digital twins, how they're changing sectors, and why they're essential for companies that want to be prepared for the future.

Let's explore the revolutionary potential of digital twins and see how they are spurring innovation in the real world.

Stay tuned!

## Benefits of Digital Twins

[**Digital twin**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin)technology is increasingly critical in our pursuit of efficient operations. It offers substantial returns on investment by increasing productivity and cutting expenses.

So, let’s discuss the benefits of the digital twin one by one.

![Benefits of Digital Twins](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/1600x1000/fcbe0a9f83/benefits-of-digital-twins.webp)

![Benefits of Digital Twins](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/1600x1000/fcbe0a9f83/benefits-of-digital-twins.webp)

### **1. Improved Product Design and Innovation**

Digital twins are powerful instruments for promoting product design and innovation. By making virtual prints, businesses can mimic and evaluate new designs in different situations without requiring actual prototypes. This method dramatically reduces expenses and resource consumption while also expediting the design process.

Consider[**digital twins in the automotive industry**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-automotive-industry). Tesla has streamlined vehicle design and production by utilizing digital twins. They imitate how cars would behave in various scenarios to guarantee top efficiency and security prior to the construction of any actual automobiles. Businesses that want to stay competitive and develop constantly must understand the[**use cases of digital twins in the automotive industry**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-use-cases-in-automotive-industry).

Conversely, Boeing models airplane performance using digital twins in the aerospace industry to improve fuel economy and aerodynamics without the need for several expensive and laborious physical tests.

These illustrations show how digital twins help businesses develop more quickly and precisely. This results in finished goods that are not just creative but also dependable and well-made. This capacity is crucial for companies trying to remain competitive in quickly evolving markets.

### **2. Predictive Maintenance and Downtime Reduction**

Digital twins are leading the way in the revolution of predictive maintenance, which is revolutionizing business processes. With digital twins, businesses can reduce unexpected downtime by anticipating breakdowns before they happen by simulating actual systems in a virtual environment.

Digital twins, for example, are used by Airbus to track the health of its aircraft and identify possible problems before they impact operations. This proactive strategy improves aircraft availability and reduces maintenance costs, which has a direct influence on safety and operational continuity.

Similar to this,[**digital twins in the energy industry**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twins-in-energy) are useful for wind farms. Businesses like GE Digital use them to plan maintenance and anticipate equipment breakdowns. This strategic vision shows how digital twins are essential for contemporary businesses.

This is for businesses looking to maximize operational efficiency and dependability by dramatically reducing disruption and guaranteeing continuous energy production.

### **3. Optimized Supply Chains and Logistics**

[**Digital twins in supply chain management**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-supply-chain-management) become dynamic when they are used to replace conventional logistics. Companies can model their supply chain operations with extreme accuracy using digital twins, which helps them plan ahead for delays, improve their routes, and better allocate their resources.

DHL, for instance, uses digital twin technology to track its shipping processes, improving visibility and enabling early detection and resolution of possible delays and bottlenecks. With the flexibility to adapt in real-time, they can guarantee more efficient operations and timely delivery. This helps maintain customer satisfaction and cut expenses.

BMW also uses digital twins to model its manufacturing line and supply chain setups. This program helps them minimize waste, streamline processes, and forecast results in various scenarios. In the modern marketplace, when supply chain reliability and effectiveness are more important than ever, these kinds of innovations are essential for retaining competitive advantage.

**Suggested read:** Benefits of[**Digital Twin in Healthcare**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-healthcare): What It Is, What It Does?

### **4. Enhanced Operational Efficiency**

Real-time system monitoring lowers operating expenses and interruptions by enabling the early detection and resolution of possible problems. This proactive maintenance strategy increases the overall dependability of business processes while also extending the life of the machinery. Thus, operational effectiveness is improved.

Imagine if an energy business could modify production rates depending on real-time supply and demand data or if a manufacturing plant could anticipate a breakdown before it occurs.

That is how digital twins work.

Businesses may make better decisions more quickly with the help of digital twins. Therefore, they offer[**real-time integration in digital twins,**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/real-time-integration-in-digital-twins)which gives digital counterparts to physical assets.

Siemens, for example, employs digital twins to track and improve wind turbine performance throughout the world. This results in lower downtime and higher energy production.

### **5. Increased Energy Efficiency**

Digital twins are an incredible instrument for increasing energy utilization, which is essential for companies trying to cut expenses and maximize energy utilization. By modeling real-world systems, digital twins allow businesses to find errors and make changes that boost energy use without affecting actual processes.

The domain of facilities management is an exquisite example of this use. Siemens has integrated digital twin technology into its building management system to achieve dynamic energy optimization. Their solutions can significantly reduce the use of energy when incorporated into actual construction operations. They do this by simulating energy flows and testing enhanced efficiency in a simulated setting.

Similarly, in the industrial sector, businesses such as Chevron optimize the energy consumption of massive machines and networks through the[**use of digital twins**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-use-cases). By analyzing operating trends and sensor data, Chevron is able to forecast peak energy use and make real-time process adjustments to maximize performance.

### **6. Enhanced Safety and Risk Management**

[**Digital twin security**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-security)considerably improves security requirements and risk management across a variety of industries. This is possible through the provision of a flexible platform for continuous evaluation and modeling. Due to this technology, firms may take preventative measures by anticipating possible safety hazards and operational vulnerabilities before they materialize.

Companies such as AECOM, which works in the construction sector, employ digital twins to mimic building pressures and unexpected events. They do this to ensure structural safety and be ready for emergency reactions. This preventative measure guarantees public and employee safety in addition to structural integrity.

Shell is improving the safety of its offshore oil rigs in the energy industry. It is possible to use digital twins to optimize maintenance plans and anticipate equipment problems. As a result, there are fewer accidents and environmental risks, highlighting the critical role that digital twins play.

### **7. Improved Customer and Client Engagement**

Improving client and customer involvement is essential for companies looking to strengthen bonds and raise consumer satisfaction. Digital twins, which provide individualized and engaging experiences, can help uniquely accomplish this. Thus,[**digital twins enhance customer experience**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-customer-experience). While they are actually built, these digital copies let customers view and engage with construction projects or products in real-time.

In the real estate industry, for example, businesses such as Lendlease are utilizing digital twins to provide potential clients with a virtual tour of their homes. This not only aids in marketing but also dramatically increases client happiness and participation by enabling purchasers to request specific features and instantly observe the potential results.

By utilizing digital twins to enable online vehicle customization, Audi has elevated customer interaction in the automobile sector. In a virtual setting, customers can alter parts, choose colors, and add devices, which not only makes the experience more unique but also increases their likelihood of making a purchase.

### **8. Customization and Personalization of Products**

Digital twins provide significant competitive benefits by revolutionizing the way firms approach product customization and modification. Businesses can customize products to meet the unique needs of each consumer without incurring the high costs and lengthy lead times associated with conventional approaches. This is through digital simulation of real goods and how they interact.

Nike’s application of digital twin technology for personalizing athletic apparel is a prime illustration of this. Nike employs digital twins to replicate the look and feel of shoes, while consumers can choose the design features and materials. This helps to save waste and simplify manufacturing while also improving client satisfaction by meeting their exact needs.

Parallel to this, the automotive sector, headed by BMW, uses digital twins to provide customers with customized alternatives for car components and settings. This method allows precise demands to be met and provides a unique client experience without interfering with the production procedure.

## FAQs

### 1. Digital Twin Technology and its practical uses now and in the future.

Digital twin technology is revolutionizing industries by making it possible to simulate and analyze physical possessions in real-time. These days, it's employed to improve productivity in operations, industrial process optimization, and predictive maintenance. Future uses for it include sophisticated supply chain optimization, patient-specific models for personalized healthcare, and infrastructure management in smart cities. By combining AI and IoT, digital twins can completely transform how companies plan, monitor, and develop.

### 2. What are the top benefits of digital twin technology in the construction industry?

The construction industry can profit significantly from digital twin technology.

Improved Project Planning: Optimizes workflows by simulating construction processes.

Better Risk Management: Reduces expenses and delays by seeing any problems early.

Predictive maintenance monitors the condition of the infrastructure to ensure prompt repairs and a longer asset life.

Sustainability: Reduces waste and carbon footprints while optimizing resource use.

Cooperation and Communication: Enhances decision-making and project transparency by giving stakeholders access to a single digital model.

### 3. What are the Challenges of digital twin technology?

The following are the challenges involved in digital twin technology:

High Implementation Costs: A substantial financial commitment is needed to develop and implement digital twins.

Complex Data Integration: Combining information from several sources can be difficult and time-consuming.

Skill Gaps: Adoption and efficacy may be hampered by a lack of knowledge of digital twin technology.

Cybersecurity Risks: One of the biggest challenges is preventing sensitive data breaches.

Scalability Problems: Modifying digital twins for extensive operations or numerous assets can be complex.

Digital twin technology has a bright future, with developments anticipated in many sectors.

Improved real-time data processing and predictive analytics using AI and IoT integration.

Growth in Smart Cities: Improving energy management and urban infrastructure.

Personalized healthcare: Customized patient care by using virtual human system modeling.

Digital twins will be a key component of efficiency and creativity in the upcoming years.

# 5 Big Benefits of Digital Twins in Governmental Infrastructure Projects

Oct 17, 2023 | [Virtual Design & Construction](https://ineight.com/category/blog/virtual-design-construction/)

[Digital twins](https://ineight.com/process-solutions/building-information-modeling-digital-twin/) are not a new concept. Since first being employed during a compromised Apollo mission in 1970, this advanced construction [technology](https://ineight.com/products/ineight-model/) has moved beyond aerospace use, demonstrating value in industries like healthcare, automotive and construction. While it might seem they’re in the relative infancy of their adoption in our industry, they’ve been experiencing a growth spurt in recent years and are poised to continue on that trajectory.

[Advanced construction technologies have been earmarked](https://www.constructiondive.com/news/construction-tech-praise-110-million-infrastructure-bill-push-for-more/610126/) to receive tech investment funding from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) — highlighting just how critical such technologies are. Adopting these digital tools serves multiple purposes, not the least of which is to help capital projects meet their timeline and budget expectations and to better manage and execute the growing sophistication of modern construction.

For construction managers overseeing environmental and government infrastructure projects, digital twin technology [delivers benefits](https://ineight.com/blog/how-digital-twins-benefit-contractors-and-owners/) that few other advanced technologies can.

## Enhanced visualization aids in design validation and error/conflict detection

As a “digitally living” version of the built asset, digital twins allow you to take virtual tours of the project before and during construction. Its [enhanced visualization](https://ineight.com/blog/a-powerful-combination-asset-management-and-the-digital-twin/) creates an immersive experience.

Because it’s so highly detailed, stakeholders can explore every aspect of the structure from various angles and perspectives. You can spot potential structural or safety issues and design conflicts and address them before they become built into the asset. And you can get a sense of how a project will fit and function within its immediate surroundings.

These virtual walkthroughs allow government and regulatory representatives to see how well a project is meeting their strict contractual requirements and adhering to environmental regulations. With government funding often paying for such projects, there’s a financial incentive to prove adherence to those requirements, and digital twins do just that.

## Minimized environmental impact through compliance tracking

Building sustainably and with the least environmental impact is becoming more the rule than the exception.

Digital twins allow you to experiment with scenarios and evaluate their effects so you can ask critical questions such as: Do any proposed designs or changes negatively affect the environment? What adjustments can be made to minimize them? On the flip side, are any environmental elements — sunlight direction, wind patterns or terrain, for example — shown to impact the structure in a way that necessitates modifications? How will any of those changes or elements alter energy costs to operate the asset? How will seasonal conditions influence building performance?

This proactive approach to determining design scenarios that are environmentally friendly can provide assurance of compliance with regulations.

## Improved transparency for project owners, agencies and the public

The transparency into the virtual asset itself — the walkthroughs, the flyovers, the rotation models — shows how it looks from the inside out, all around, and within its physical setting. And there’s the transparency into how the asset will likely perform under specific conditions and over time once it’s in operation. Both provide a broader understanding of a project’s scope, purpose, and reciprocal relationship with occupants and the environment.

Making the project so accessible is a solid way of fostering trust from project owners, regulatory agencies, government representatives and the community, who all have a stake in the success of the built asset.

## Reduced rework, reduced costs

Simulating and visualizing the whole project before construction begins creates a prime opportunity to evaluate it thoroughly.

On the one hand, assessing the project with a digital twin can validate that the design works and that project requirements will be met. On the other hand, the process can help reveal physical clashes, design flaws and conflicts, and environmental obstacles that would have otherwise found their way into the physical asset — and wound up as rework activities. Discovering and addressing them in advance can head off that rework and the associated wasted costs that go with it: replacement materials (potentially at a last-minute higher rate), labor to tear down and fix the error, disposal, and so on.

## More efficient facilities management

Digital twins [continue offering value](https://ineight.com/blog/vdc-and-digital-twins-facility-management-in-project-controls/) long after the last construction worker has gone home, playing a pivotal role in [making facilities management more efficient](https://ineight.com/blog/beyond-the-model-managing-your-completed-assets-with-digital-twins/) and less cumbersome. They conduct behind-the-scenes monitoring of equipment and systems, relaying real-time data about how individual components are performing. What is performing well? What is due for regular maintenance? Is anything showing signs of needing some preventative maintenance? Does anything need a repair, replacement or upgrade? This keeps everything in working order while reducing the risk of damage and breakdowns, whose downtime can have broader, costlier implications for the asset. Overall, digital twins extend the asset’s functional health and safety.

Are environmental or government infrastructure projects currently or projected to be in your portfolio? Digital twin technology, like [InEight Digital Twin](https://ineight.com/process-solutions/building-information-modeling-digital-twin/), is a must-have as part of your arsenal of advanced construction technology. [Learn how InEight can support your special capital projects](https://ineight.com/request-a-demo).

enriched_points.point_title: Digital twins are reshaping infrastructure management
enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins create virtual replicas of physical infrastructure, enabling enhanced visualization and real-time monitoring for improved decision-making and project management.
By leveraging IoT and real-time data integration, digital twins optimize operational processes, allowing for immediate adjustments and proactive issue resolution.

enriched_points.point_title: Predictive capabilities lead to cost savings
enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins facilitate predictive maintenance, reducing unexpected downtimes and enabling more efficient resource allocation, which translates to significant cost savings.
Emerging technologies, such as AI and augmented reality, are set to enhance digital twin capabilities, driving innovation and efficiency across infrastructure sectors.
